summary: Drew DeVault announces the first pre-release of aerc, a terminal-based email client he's been developing. The project's pace accelerated after he began working on free software full time, funded by donations. aerc differentiates itself from mutt with vim-like keybindings, more reliable networking, and an embedded terminal emulator that allows multitasking within the client. He outlines future plans including version control integration, calendar support, and additional email backends.
key_points: aerc 0.1.0 is the first pre-release of a terminal email client that improves on mutt with better networking and vim-style keybindings
The embedded terminal emulator is the key differentiator, enabling users to compose emails, review patches with syntax highlighting, and run arbitrary programs in tabs
Development accelerated directly because of DeVault's transition to full-time free software work funded by donations
The email-centric workflow integrates tightly with git/hg for patch-based development, reflecting DeVault's philosophy on software collaboration
money_quotes: If you're coming from mutt, you'll appreciate its more efficient & reliable networking, a keybinding system closer to vims, and embedded terminal emulator allowing you to compose emails and read new ones at the same time.
Writing emails in vim, manipulating git & hg repositories, playing nethack to kill some time… all stuff you never realized your email client was missing.
Want to run Weechat in an aerc tab? Just like that, aerc has a chat client!

The embedded terminal emulator also allows us convenient access to nearby git repositories for running tests against incoming patches, pushing the changes once accepted, or anything else you might want to do.
After my transition to working on free software full time allowed me to spend more time on more projects, I was able to invest considerably more time into aerc.
key_insight: An embedded terminal emulator transforms an email client from a single-purpose tool into a composable development environment where patch review, version control, and communication converge.
